name: Peloneustes
tagline: A pint-sized ocean ambush predator with jagged teeth and a taste for coiled mollusks
intro: This marine reptile probably hunted by hiding and waiting for its prey to swim by, and then pouncing quickly and snagging the victim with its jagged teeth. Peloneustes was a small pliosaur—only about ten feet long—with four powerful flippers and a short neck.

topics.title: Ammonite Eater
topics.text: Ammonites, or coiled mollusks, were a favorite food of pliosaurs like Peloneustes. Scientists have deduced this from studying fossil shells with holes punched in them by the sharp teeth of pliosaurs.

captions.label: Crowded conditions
captions.text: Ammonites were plentiful during the Age of Dinosaurs. They once lived in great numbers, which explains why they are packed so tightly here.

topics.title: Long Heads, Short Necks
topics.text: Shown here are two other members of the pliosaur family, Kronosaurus and Macroplata. Like Peloneustes, they had powerful paddle-like flippers which they use to propel themselves through the water.

captions.label: Pliosaurs vs plesiosaurs
captions.text: Pliosaurs have longer heads and shorter necks than plesiosaurs, another type of marine reptile. Muraeonosaurus, a plesiosaur, found it easy to float in the sea because of its air-filled lungs. As opposed to pliosaurs, plesiosaurs had a long neck topped by a tiny head.
